label: delphi-informed-p3
system:
You are performing one specific cognitive operation as an isolated component of a legal-exploration pipeline. You never see the other components' outputs, and nobody sees yours until a separate neutral aggregation step. Ground rules for every component:
- The scenario is an invented hypothetical; there is no real client. Your output goes to a licensed California attorney who will do the actual legal reasoning. You generate angles to pursue and things to verify — not advice to be relied on.
- Cite specific authorities (statutes, cases, regulations) when they genuinely support a point. Every citation is automatically flagged UNVERIFIED for human checking, so cite what you believe is correct, but never fabricate quotations or pinpoint holdings you are unsure of. When unsure, write "verify whether X holds Y" instead of asserting it.
- Perform ONLY the operation described below. Do not produce a general-purpose legal memo.

Operation: you are ONE independent panelist in a Delphi elicitation. There is no
discussion; you answer alone. Give your honest probability, not a diplomatic one.
Do not hedge toward 50 to be safe — commit to your analysis.
prompt:
## Facts (invented hypothetical — no real parties)

The Sierra Vista Municipal Water District (invented) serves ~140,000 people in a chronically drought-stressed service area. After a rate study and a majority-protest hearing that drew protests from ~9% of parcels, the board adopted a "Water Resilience Surcharge": $0.85 per hundred cubic feet on residential usage above Tier 2 (the surcharge falls on roughly the top 22% of residential users), projected to raise $4.1M/year into a restricted fund spent as follows: 55% on turf-replacement and high-efficiency-appliance rebates that prioritize low-income customers; 30% on debt service for a recycled-water plant; 15% on "water ambassador" conservation-education staff.

The rate study concludes that high-tier consumption imposes marginal supply costs roughly 1.6× base-tier costs (peaking capacity, drought-reserve purchases), but it derives the $0.85 figure in part from what it calls "conservation-incentive pricing" rather than strictly from cost allocation, and the district concedes the rebate program mostly benefits customers other than those paying the surcharge. A ratepayer association has sued, alleging the surcharge exceeds the proportional cost of service attributable to the payers' parcels in violation of article XIII D, section 6(b) of the California Constitution, citing Capistrano Taxpayers Association v. City of San Juan Capistrano. The district defends the structure under its conservation mandate (Cal. Const. art. X, § 2; Water Code § 375) and argues that, in the alternative, the surcharge is not a property-related fee at all.

## The question

Does the Water Resilience Surcharge violate article XIII D, section 6(b)'s cost-of-service and proportionality requirements? How does Capistrano apply to a tier surcharge that is partly cost-justified (1.6×) and partly incentive-priced, with proceeds partially redistributed as rebates to non-payers? Can article X, section 2's conservation mandate be reconciled with 218, or does 218 win? Is there a colorable argument the surcharge is a tax requiring voter approval (Prop 26), or a non-property-related charge? What is the litigation exposure (refunds, fees) and how could the structure be repaired?

## Proposition to assess

"A court would invalidate the Water Resilience Surcharge, in whole or in substantial part, as violating the cost-of-service requirements of California Constitution article XIII D, section 6(b)."

## Verified-authority annex

Estimate the probability (0–100) that the proposition is correct, with your reasoning. rationales = your independent load-bearing reasons, one sentence each. authorities = authorities you actually rely on. biggest_uncertainty = the single thing you are least sure of. Treat the annex as evidence about the authorities, not as anyone's opinion on the answer.

Analytical starting point for you: Begin from enforcement realities: who would sue, what remedies exist, what actually happens in practice.
text: {"probability":70,"confidence":"medium","position_summary":"More likely than not that a reviewing court invalidates the Water Resilience Surcharge in at least substantial part — most probably the revenue-use/cross-subsidy piece (the 55% rebate allocation benefiting non-payors) and/or the increment of the $0.85 rate attributable to \"conservation-incentive pricing\" beyond the district's own proven 1.6x cost differential — even though the district's genuine marginal-cost study gives it a materially stronger record than the agencies that lost in Capistrano and Palmdale.","rationales":["The rate study's own admission that $0.85 derives 'in part' from conservation-incentive pricing rather than strictly cost allocation closely tracks the defect Capistrano (235 Cal.App.4th 1493) condemned — tiered/inclining rates must be justified by actual cost-of-service differences, not conservation goals as an independent pricing rationale.","Article XIII D §6(b)(5) places the burden of proof on the agency in any enforcement action, and courts applying rigorous (non-deferential) review to that burden have struck down rate structures on records far more ambiguous than one containing an explicit non-cost pricing admission.","The district's concession that ~55% of surcharge revenue funds rebates mostly benefiting customers other than the payors raises a distinct cross-subsidization problem — revenue collected from one class arguably cannot exceed or be diverted to fund benefits primarily for a different class — and this alone plausibly satisfies 'substantial tiers) has an actual engineering/marginal-cost study showing a real 1.6x cost differential tied to peaking capacity and drought-reserve purchases — the kind of cost linkage Capistrano itself suggested could validate a tiered structure — giving a court a basis to sever and preserve a cost-justified rate rather than void the whole surcharge.","Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil (39 Cal.4th 205) forecloses the district's fallback 'not a property-related fee' argument for volumetric water delivery charges, meaning the dispute will almost certainly be resolved on strict Article XIII D §6(b) cost-of-service grounds rather than escaping heightened scrutiny altogether, which increases the odds some portion of the surcharge is found non-compliant."],"biggest_uncertainty":"Whether a court would treat the admitted \"conservation-incentive pricing\" component and the rebate cross-subsidy as grounds to void the entire $0.85 surcharge/fund allocation, or would instead order a narrower reformation (paring the rate back to the 1.6x cost-justified level and/or redirecting rebate spending) that a reviewer might not characterize as invalidation \"in substantial part.\""}

data.position_summary: More likely than not that a reviewing court invalidates the Water Resilience Surcharge in at least substantial part — most probably the revenue-use/cross-subsidy piece (the 55% rebate allocation benefiting non-payors) and/or the increment of the $0.85 rate attributable to "conservation-incentive pricing" beyond the district's own proven 1.6x cost differential — even though the district's genuine marginal-cost study gives it a materially stronger record than the agencies that lost in Capistrano and Palmdale.
rationales: The rate study's own admission that $0.85 derives 'in part' from conservation-incentive pricing rather than strictly cost allocation closely tracks the defect Capistrano (235 Cal.App.4th 1493) condemned — tiered/inclining rates must be justified by actual cost-of-service differences, not conservation goals as an independent pricing rationale.

Article XIII D §6(b)(5) places the burden of proof on the agency in any enforcement action, and courts applying rigorous (non-deferential) review to that burden have struck down rate structures on records far more ambiguous than one containing an explicit non-cost pricing admission.

The district's concession that ~55% of surcharge revenue funds rebates mostly benefiting customers other than the payors raises a distinct cross-subsidization problem — revenue collected from one class arguably cannot exceed or be diverted to fund benefits primarily for a different class — and this alone plausibly satisfies 'substantial part' invalidation even if the cost-justified core of the tier differential survives.

Cutting the other way, Sierra Vista (unlike Palmdale's water-budget scheme or Capistrano's undifferentiated tiers) has an actual engineering/marginal-cost study showing a real 1.6x cost differential tied to peaking capacity and drought-reserve purchases — the kind of cost linkage Capistrano itself suggested could validate a tiered structure — giving a court a basis to sever and preserve a cost-justified rate rather than void the whole surcharge.

Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil (39 Cal.4th 205) forecloses the district's fallback 'not a property-related fee' argument for volumetric water delivery charges, meaning the dispute will almost certainly be resolved on strict Article XIII D §6(b) cost-of-service grounds rather than escaping heightened scrutiny altogether, which increases the odds some portion of the surcharge is found non-compliant.
data.biggest_uncertainty: Whether a court would treat the admitted "conservation-incentive pricing" component and the rebate cross-subsidy as grounds to void the entire $0.85 surcharge/fund allocation, or would instead order a narrower reformation (paring the rate back to the 1.6x cost-justified level and/or redirecting rebate spending) that a reviewer might not characterize as invalidation "in substantial part."
